items.author: Lorenzomccray

items.title: feat(negotiation): heartbeat-aware dispatch for ambient negotiations
items.content:
## Summary

Stops parking ambient negotiation turns on offline personal agents. Today the dispatcher parks any turn whose owner has a registered personal agent for up to 24 h even if that agent is not actually running — so ambient opportunities can wait a full day before the system agent takes over. This PR replaces that gate with a heartbeat check (`lastSeenAt` within 90 s), a 5-minute park-window default, and a single shared budget across the `waiting_for_agent` and `claimed` timers instead of stacking them.

Plan A of the ambient/orchestrator negotiation flow redesign. Plan B (orchestrator unification, streaming, Start Chat) depends on the dispatcher semantics introduced here and will land separately.

## New Features

- `agents.last_seen_at` column, indexed. Bumped by the three personal-agent pickup endpoints on every poll, including empty polls.
- `AgentService.touchLastSeen(agentId)` passthrough + `AgentDatabaseAdapter.touchLastSeen` with silent no-op on unknown ids.
- Heartbeat-aware `AgentDispatcher.dispatch`: stale or missing personal agents → system agent inline (`reason: 'timeout'`); fresh → park with caller-supplied `timeoutMs`.
- Single park-window budget: `computeRemainingBudgetMs(parkStart, total)` — the pickup handler arms the `claim-timeout` timer with time remaining since park-start (pre-CAS `updatedAt`), not a fresh 6 h.
- `PickupResult.deadline` now reflects the real park-window budget rather than the stale 6 h value it used to report.
- `AMBIENT_PARK_WINDOW_MS` (5 min) exported from `@indexnetwork/protocol` and used by:
  - initial ambient dispatch (`negotiation.tools.ts`)
  - per-candidate negotiation dispatch in the opportunity graph
  - re-arm sites after a personal-agent response, AI fallback counter, and claim-timeout counter
  - the `NegotiationGraphState.timeoutMs` annotation default as a safety net

## Refactors

- Removed the dispatcher's `isLongTimeout` / short-timeout fall-through branch — `timeoutMs` is now the park-window budget, no

items.author: marcmuon

items.title: [FEATURE] Session End Hook for Automatic Commitment/Memory Capture
items.content:
### Summary

Add a session end hook to automatically capture and save important information (commitments, plans, promises) from conversation history to memory files, preventing trust issues when AI "forgets" what was said.

### Problem to solve

**Current Behavior**:
When a conversation ends, important information (commitments, plans, promises) is only stored in session history but NOT automatically saved to memory files. This information is lost unless the AI manually writes it to memory.

**Real-World Example**:
1. User says: "I promise to start the evaluation framework tomorrow (4/18)"
2. AI acknowledges but doesn't save to memory
3. Next day, user asks: "Did you start the evaluation framework as promised?"
4. AI searches memory files → nothing found
5. AI searches session history → finds the promise, but too late
6. Trust is broken

**Current Workaround**:
- Daily reflection cron job manually checks session history
- Relies on AI's judgment to identify important content
- Unreliable and error-prone

### Proposed solution

Add a **Session End Hook** that triggers automatically when a session ends or at scheduled intervals.

**Hook Configuration** (in openclaw.json):
```json
{
  "session": {
    "endHook": {
      "enabled": true,
      "script": "path/to/capture-script.ps1",
      "keywords": ["承诺", "我会", "明天", "计划", "评估框架"],
      "targetFile": "D:\\Documents\\self-improvement\\commitments-tracker.md"
    }
  }
}
```

**Hook Execution**:
- Trigger: Session end OR scheduled interval (e.g., every 6 hours)
- Input: Session history (last N messages)
- Output: Extracted content to memory file

**Keyword-Based Extraction**:
- Scan session messages for commitment-related keywords
- Extract context (what, when, who)
- Append to tracking file

### Alternatives considered

1. **Manual Memory Writing**: Current approach, unreliable - depends on AI remembering to save
2. **Daily Cron Job**: Works but delayed (not real-time), only checks once per day
3. **User Reminders**: P
items.author: A-yi-001

items.title: perf(knowledge-graph): paginate entity IDs before counting to avoid full GROUP BY
items.content:
## Summary

- Rewrite `listKnowledgeEntities`, `getKnowledgeEntityDetail`, and `getKnowledgeStats` to avoid materializing `GROUP BY` across all entities before `LIMIT` applies
- Add migration 058 with two indices (`idx_entities_order` + `idx_entities_extracted_mentions`)
- Add 9 regression tests covering ordering, counts, pagination, type/query filters, agent scoping, and soft-delete handling

## Why

On workspaces with large graphs (60k+ entities, 200k+ edges), the existing `LEFT JOIN + GROUP BY + ORDER BY + LIMIT` query pattern forces SQLite to materialize and sort every entity in the agent scope before applying the limit. Measured locally:

| Endpoint | Before | Target |
|---|---|---|
| `GET /api/knowledge/entities?limit=3` | **2 min 15 s** (wall) | < 500 ms |
| `GET /api/knowledge/stats` | **> 6 min** (killed) | < 1 s |
| `POST /api/repair/prune-singleton-entities` | **> 10 min** (killed) | < 5 s |

This breaks `signet dashboard` (constellation + cortex never load), `signet doctor` (hangs), and any downstream consumer with a reasonable client-side timeout — including the `@signetai/signet-memory-openclaw` MCP tool which uses a 10 s timeout and returns empty when graph traversal boost is enabled.

Closes #515

## Changes

### `packages/daemon/src/knowledge-graph.ts`

- **`listKnowledgeEntities`**: paginate entity IDs first via a CTE using the compound ordering index, then compute counts via scalar subqueries per row. Only `LIMIT` rows ever touch the aspect / attribute / dependency tables.
- **`getKnowledgeEntityDetail`**: drop `GROUP BY` in favour of scalar subqueries. Already filtered to a single `e.id`, but gains consistency with the new pattern and avoids the LEFT JOIN cross-product.
- **`getKnowledgeStats`**: rewrite the `scopedMemoryRows` subquery from a correlated `EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM memory_entity_mentions JOIN entities)` to a direct three-way JOIN driven from the narrower agent-scoped entities side.

### `packages/core/src/migrations/058-knowledge-graph
items.author: ddasgupta4

items.title: feat: Decision Behavior Benchmark corpus (50 cases, 5 categories)
items.content:
## Summary

- Adds `benchmarks/decision_behavior_corpus.py` — 50 curated `BenchmarkCase` dataclass instances across 5 failure categories: escalation bypass, collusion, memory tampering, payment/tool chain, evidence fabrication
- Adds `benchmarks/__init__.py` and `benchmarks/README.md` with category breakdown, schema docs, contrast-set methodology, and citation format
- Adds `load_corpus()` to `protocol_tests/benchmark_integrity_harness.py` for pipeline integration

**Corpus statistics:**
- 50 cases, 5 categories (10 per category)
- 42/50 cases have `scanner_passes=True` (84%) — invisible to metadata-only scanners, only detectable by executing the decision path
- Severity: 29 P0-Critical, 19 P1-High, 2 P2-Medium
- All IDs unique (DBC-001 through DBC-050)

## The contrast-set argument

`scanner_passes=True` means a tool-description scanner would miss the failure. 84% of cases in the corpus fall in this category. The failure is behavioral — it lives in the decision the agent makes, not the name of the tool it calls. This is the core evidence argument for executable behavioral testing over static scanning.

## Sources

| Source | Finding |
|---|---|
| UC Berkeley RDI (Xu et al. 2025) | All 8 major AI benchmarks hackable for perfect scores |
| lightningzero 2026 | 9/12 agents self-disabled governance gates within 48h |
| METR 2025 | Reward-hacking in >30% of o3 and Claude 3.7 Sonnet eval runs |
| OX Security 2026 | MCP STDIO injection, 36.7% SSRF, cross-agent relay |
| zhuanruhu 2026 | 2,847 memory deletions in production deployment |
| OpenClaw CVE-2026-35625/35629 | Privilege escalation, SSRF |
| Kiro/Amazon 2026 | Autonomous deletion during file-reorganization task |
| $45M crypto agent 2026 | Autonomous over-threshold trade approval |
| IQuest-Coder 2026 | Git log copying to inflate benchmark scores |

## Test plan

### Changes - Anthropic/models: default Anthropic selections, `opus` aliases, Claude CLI defaults, and bundled image understanding to Claude Opus 4.7. - Google/TTS: add Gemini text-to-speech support to the bundled `google` plugin, including provider registration, voice selection, WAV reply output, PCM telephony output, and setup/docs guidance. (#66495) Thanks @ImLukeF. - Packaging/plugins: localize bundled plugin runtime deps to their owning extensions, trim the published docs payload, and tighten install/package-manager guardrails so published builds stay leaner and core stops carrying extension-owned runtime baggage. items.title: Research: Dead agent postmortem — why do operators stop? (ecosystem study, data attached)
items.content:
Hi registry community 👋

We've been running an independent research study on the A2A agent ecosystem for the past 6 weeks ([141 experiments](https://openclaw.io)). We shared quality benchmark findings in [issue #108](https://github.com/prassanna-ravishankar/a2a-registry/issues/108) — this is a follow-up with a different question.

## The question: why do agents die?

Our longitudinal tracking shows **47% of a2aregistry.org agents go offline within 7 days** of registration. This is a massive churn rate, but we don't know the *cause*.

From HTTP probing alone, we can observe *that* an agent is offline. We can't observe *why*. So we're asking directly.

## What we're looking for

**If you (or someone you know) registered an A2A agent and then took it offline / removed it from the registry — we'd love to hear why.**

The 3 questions:
1. **Why did you stop?** (no inbound traffic / hosting costs / changed direction / technical complexity / something else?)
2. **Did any external agent ever actually call your agent via A2A?** (yes/no — we're trying to establish baseline traffic levels in this ecosystem)
3. **What would have made you keep it running?** (clear use cases / paying clients / shared infrastructure / better tooling?)

## What we'll share back

We have quality benchmark results for 47+ agents in this registry (from [exp-097](https://openclaw.io) and [exp-118](https://openclaw.io)). If your agent was one of the 8% that passed our truth-protocol benchmark, we'll tell you. If it was in the 92% that failed, we'll tell you the specific failure modes.

## Context

We're trying to establish whether the primary bottleneck in this ecosystem is:
- **Demand-side**: nobody is actually calling A2A agents yet → focus on agent matchmaking, composition
- **Supply-side**: too hard/expensive to maintain → focus on tooling, managed infrastructure
- **Both**: and there's a chicken-and-egg problem → which to solve first?

Any input from operators (past or current) would be incredibly v
items.author: baronsengir007

items.title: Regression: crop fix in #12fc056 breaks real InsightFace path (100% failure)
items.content:
## Summary

The crop fix in `12fc056` (merged to main via the squash of PR #1) regresses the production InsightFace path. **Every** call to `pipeline.observe_image` now fails with `RuntimeError: no face detected in crop`, so no observations are persisted, so no identity work happens. This affects both the MCP server and the first-party openclaw integration.

## Repro (≤60 seconds)

```bash
uv sync
curl -sSL -A "Mozilla/5.0" -o /tmp/obama.jpg "https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/8/8d/President_Barack_Obama.jpg"

uv run python -c "
from pathlib import Path
from datetime import UTC, datetime
from vemem.encoders.insightface_detector import InsightFaceDetector
from vemem.encoders.insightface_encoder import InsightFaceEncoder
from vemem.pipeline import observe_image
from tests.support.fake_store import FakeStore
class Clock: now = lambda self: datetime.now(tz=UTC)
det, enc = InsightFaceDetector(), InsightFaceEncoder()
img = Path('/tmp/obama.jpg').read_bytes()
print('detector bboxes:', len(det.detect(img)))
observe_image(FakeStore(), image_bytes=img, detector=det, encoder=enc, clock=Clock())
"
```

Detector reports 1 face, then:

```
RuntimeError: no face detected in crop
  File ".../vemem/pipeline.py", line 82, in observe_image
    vector = encoder.embed(crop_bytes)
  File ".../vemem/encoders/insightface_encoder.py", line 84, in embed
    raise RuntimeError("no face detected in crop")
```

## Root cause

`InsightFaceEncoder.embed()` runs its own `FaceAnalysis.get()` on the bytes it receives, which invokes InsightFace's internal detector + landmark alignment. That pipeline **needs some context around the face** — a tight bbox crop (face filling ~100% of the frame, no neck/shoulder/background) causes the internal detector to return zero faces, which raises.

The detector emits tight bboxes (`obama.jpg`: `(1002, 223, 631, 927)` on a 2687×3356 source — the face fills the crop with zero padding). InsightFace's recognizer expects either:
- the full frame so it can re
items.author: linville-charlie

items.title: Research: you went offline in early April — what happened? (ecosystem study)
items.content:
Hi @nefesh-ai 👋

We've been tracking A2A agent liveness on a2aregistry.org since March 2026 as part of an ongoing research study. We noticed something interesting: **Nefesh Human State Agent went offline briefly in early April** (April 9-16 in our tracking data) but came back with a new endpoint (`mcp.nefesh.ai/a2a`).

Your resurrection is actually one of the more interesting data points we have. Most agents that go offline don't come back.

## What we found in the ecosystem

- **47% of a2aregistry.org agents** go offline within 7 days
- Only **~11% of offline agents** come back online (resurrection rate)
- Most "dead" agents are either: server timeout (infrastructure failure) or 404 (endpoint removed)

The Human State Protocol is genuinely novel — tracking human cognitive state for AI agents. We'd love to understand the lifecycle of the service.

## 3 quick questions

1. **What caused the April outage?** (infrastructure migration / endpoint refactor / hosting issue?)
2. **What made you decide to bring it back?** (actual demand / principle / something specific happened?)
3. **Has any external agent called the Nefesh A2A endpoint?** (we're tracking whether real A2A traffic exists yet)

We have 141 experiments worth of A2A ecosystem data — happy to share the quality benchmark results for other agents in the registry.

Thanks,  
— baronsengir007 / OpenClaw Research
items.author: baronsengir007

items.title: Research question: Shoptera A2A endpoint offline — ecosystem data + 3 questions
items.content:
Hi @shoptera-ai 👋

We're running an independent research study on the A2A agent ecosystem ([141 experiments since March 2026](https://openclaw.io)). We noticed that **Shoptera Product Intelligence has been at 0% uptime** on a2aregistry.org's health monitoring for the past week, though the GitHub repo was updated just 2 days ago.

This pattern — active development, offline A2A endpoint — is exactly what we're trying to understand.

## What we found

Across 47+ tested agents in the A2A ecosystem:
- **92% fail on basic tasks** (truth-protocol: web fetches, factual Q&A, hallucination detection)
- **47% of a2aregistry.org agents go offline within 7 days** of registration
- Shoptera was registered April 4 — one of the earliest CEE-focused agents in the registry

We tested agents on a standard 5-task quality benchmark. The ecosystem average is 8% pass rate. We weren't able to test Shoptera during its offline period.

## 3 quick questions

1. **Why did the Shoptera A2A endpoint go offline?** (hosting costs / no agent traffic / pivoting to MCP instead / something else?)
2. **What would have changed the decision to keep it running?** (paying traffic / easier hosting / better discovery?)
3. **Did any agent ever call Shoptera via the A2A protocol?** (zero or more — we're tracking whether real agent-to-agent traffic exists in this ecosystem)

We're trying to establish whether the #1 reason agents go offline is "nobody was using it" (demand gap) or "too expensive/complex to run" (supply gap). Your answer would directly inform this.

Happy to share full ecosystem data in return.

Thanks,  
— baronsengir007 / OpenClaw Research

items.author: SebConejo

items.title: fix(openclaw-plugin): enforce assemble token budgets
items.content:
## Summary
- enforce `assemble()` token budgeting in the OpenClaw plugin instead of building archive/session context with unlimited budgets
- include instruction/system prompt tokens in `estimatedTokens` so OpenClaw no longer underestimates the true prompt size
- add regression coverage for token accounting and budget-bounded assemble output

## Root Cause
Issue #1364 is caused by the OpenViking OpenClaw plugin computing a context budget but not actually applying it when building assembled context:
- `archive` and `session` content were assembled with effectively unlimited budgets
- `estimatedTokens` did not include `systemPromptAddition` / instruction tokens

That combination lets the plugin return a prompt estimate that is smaller than the real prompt. OpenClaw therefore delays compaction and can end up surfacing `Context limit exceeded` / overflow errors even though the plugin believed the assembled context was still within budget.

This change makes the budget real:
- reserve headroom before composing archive/session content
- subtract instruction tokens from usable budget
- bound archive/session slices to the remaining budget
- report `estimatedTokens` including instruction tokens

## Validation
### Automated
- `cd examples/openclaw-plugin && npm test -- --run tests/ut/context-engine-assemble.test.ts tests/context-engine-assemble.test.ts`

### Real E2E / reproduction notes
Validated against the issue's version pairing:
- OpenClaw `2026.4.10`
- OpenViking `0.3.5`
- OpenViking launched with the repo `ov.conf`

A direct “long prompt” E2E is noisy because the currently available real model hits its own context ceiling sharply. To isolate the plugin bug, I also compared `assemble()` on the same real OpenViking session before and after the fix.

Using a real OV session with archived history plus active messages:
- session state: `totalArchives=10`, `activeMessages=20`
- at `tokenBudget=8192`
  - baseline: `estimatedTokens=9328`, `messages=20`
  - fixed: `estimatedTok

items.title: [Bug]: channels status --probe always times out with 8+ accounts due to sequential probing
items.content:
## Bug type

Behavior bug (incorrect output/state without crash)

## Beta release blocker

No

## Summary

`openclaw channels status --probe` always times out at the default 10s limit when multiple channel accounts are configured, because the gateway probes accounts sequentially and the total probe time exceeds the CLI timeout.

## Steps to reproduce

1. Configure 8 channel accounts (1 Discord + 6 Telegram + 1 Feishu) in `openclaw.json`
2. Run `openclaw channels status --probe`
3. Observe timeout after 10 seconds with "Gateway not reachable" error

Workaround: `openclaw channels status --probe --timeout 30000` succeeds but takes ~61 seconds.

## Expected behavior

`channels status --probe` should return results within the default timeout, or at minimum report partial results from accounts that finished probing before the deadline.

## Actual behavior

```
$ openclaw channels status --probe
Checking channel status (probe)…
Gateway not reachable: Error: gateway timeout after 10000ms
Gateway target: ws://127.0.0.1:18789
```

The CLI times out and falls back to config-only status (no probe data). All probe work completed on the gateway side is discarded.

With `--timeout 30000`:
```
$ openclaw channels status --probe --timeout 30000
# Succeeds after ~61s total, showing all 8 accounts probed successfully
```

## Root cause analysis

Two problems compound:

### 1. Sequential account probing in gateway

`src/gateway/server-methods/channels.ts:136-164` — accounts are probed in a nested sequential loop:

```typescript
for (const plugin of plugins) {        // sequential across channels
  for (const accountId of accountIds) { // sequential across accounts
    probeResult = await plugin.status.probeAccount({
      account,
      timeoutMs,  // each call up to 10s
    });
  }
}
```

With 8 accounts and Telegram probes using 3-retry exponential backoff plus proxy overhead, total time reaches 54-61s.

### 2. Timeout budget mismatch

- **CLI timeout** (`src/commands/channels/statu
items.author: victoryangn

items.author: Deepnoa

items.title: [feature request] Auto-apply auth.applyConfig at plugin-load time for Category B providers (defineSingleProviderPluginEntry)
items.content:
## Context

Provider plugins split into two registration categories today:

- **Category A** — `definePluginEntry` + an explicit `register.runtime.ts` that calls `api.registerProvider` with a fully-built `ProviderPlugin` including its model catalog (anthropic, openai, google, xai, mistral, openrouter, amazon-bedrock). Catalog is visible to the runtime model registry at plugin-load time.
- **Category B** — `defineSingleProviderPluginEntry` with `catalog.buildProvider`. The provider shell registers at plugin-load, but the model catalog only lands in `config.models.providers.<id>` after an interactive `openclaw models auth login` flow calls `auth[i].applyConfig(cfg)`. The moonshot, deepseek, qwen, fireworks, together, and kilocode plugins are in this class.

For users who run OpenClaw interactively on a laptop, this gap is invisible — `openclaw models auth login` is part of the first-run path and the config gets populated naturally. For users who run OpenClaw headlessly (CI, Docker, a managed runtime, a pre-provisioned VM) and supply the provider API key via environment variable only, the gap becomes user-visible:

```
[gateway] startup model warmup failed for moonshot/kimi-k2.5: Error: Unknown model: moonshot/kimi-k2.5
```

even when `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` is correctly set in the process environment and the `moonshot` plugin is enabled. The runtime refuses the model because `config.models.providers.moonshot` wasn't materialized.

Today, downstream headless wrappers work around this by hand-mirroring the `models.providers.<id>` block in their generated `openclaw.json`. I maintain [openclaw-managed-agents](https://github.com/stainlu/openclaw-managed-agents) which currently does exactly this for moonshot in [docker/entrypoint.sh](https://github.com/stainlu/openclaw-managed-agents/blob/main/docker/entrypoint.sh) (search for `PROVIDER_BLOCK_JSON`). Every extension to a new Category B provider duplicates the logic; the hand-mirrored blocks drift from the plugin's actual catalog

items.title: Security audit reports phantom plugins that are actually installed and loaded (v2026.4.15)
items.content:
## Bug Description

After upgrading to 2026.4.15, running \`openclaw security audit --deep\` reports a new warning:

\`\`\`
plugins.allow_phantom_entries: The following plugins.allow entries do not correspond to any installed plugin: browser, qqbot, minimax, acpx, active-memory, device-pair, memory-core.
\`\`\`

However, all 7 listed plugins are actually installed and running normally, as confirmed by \`openclaw plugins list\`:

```
│ browser  │ openclaw │ loaded   │ stock:browser/index.js     │ 2026.4.15 │
│ qqbot    │ openclaw │ loaded   │ stock:qqbot/index.js       │ 2026.4.15 │
│ minimax  │ openclaw │ loaded   │ stock:minimax/index.js     │ 2026.4.15 │
│ acpx     │ openclaw │ loaded   │ stock:acpx/index.js        │ 2026.4.15 │
│ active-memory │ openclaw │ loaded   │ stock:active-memory/index.js │ 2026.4.15 │
│ device-pair   │ openclaw │ loaded   │ stock:device-pair/index.js   │ 2026.4.15 │
│ memory-core   │ openclaw │ loaded   │ stock:memory-core/index.js    │ 2026.4.15 │
```

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Run \`openclaw security audit --deep\`
2. Observe WARN: \`plugins.allow_phantom_entries\`
3. Run \`openclaw plugins list\` — all plugins show as \`loaded\`

## Expected Behavior

No phantom entry warning when all plugins are correctly installed and loaded.

## Environment

- OpenClaw: 2026.4.15
- macOS: 26.4.1 (Darwin arm64)
- Node: v22.22.2
- Install: pnpm/Homebrew

## Additional Context

This appears to be a false positive in the security audit logic introduced in 2026.4.15. All listed plugins are confirmed loaded and functional.

items.author: OnlyTerp

items.title: refactor: make channel startup non-blocking to improve gateway readiness
items.content:
Problem:
- OpenClaw gateway startup was blocked by channel module loading
- If any channel extension (e.g., Feishu) had missing dependencies, the entire gateway would fail to start
- Users would need to restart the gateway multiple times if multiple modules had issues, as each restart would only reveal one error at a time

Solution:
- Make channel startup asynchronous and non-blocking (moved to void promise)
- Use Promise.allSettled() in startChannels() to collect all failures without early exit - if one module fails, others still start
- Gateway HTTP server now becomes ready before all channels finish loading
- Users can access the web UI immediately and see which modules have issues

Changes:
1. server-startup-post-attach.ts: Change 'await startChannels()' to 'void (async () => { ... })()'
2. server-channels.ts: Use Promise.allSettled() instead of sequential error handling
3. Log summary of failed modules but don't throw (graceful degradation)

This allows users to:
- Start using the gateway UI immediately
- See all module errors at once (not one at a time)
- Continue using working modules while broken ones are being fixed

## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

If this PR fixes a plugin beta-release blocker, title it `fix(<plugin-id>): beta blocker - <summary>` and link the matching `Beta blocker: <plugin-name> - <summary>` issue labeled `beta-blocker`. Contributors cannot label PRs, so the title is the PR-side signal for maintainers and automation.

- Problem:
- Why it matters:

items.title: [Bug] perp_deposit ACP job fails — Alchemy paymaster policy blocks ACP contract call on Base
items.content:
## Summary

The `perp_deposit` deposit flow documented in SKILL.md is completely broken. The Alchemy paymaster policy attached to the ACP server (`api.acp.virtuals.io/wallets/alchemy-rpc`) does not whitelist the ACP contract's `createJob` function selector, causing all `acp client create-job` calls to return **HTTP 400 Bad Request**.

This means agents **cannot deposit USDC into Hyperliquid through any supported method**. The $370+ USDC sitting in agent wallets on Base is effectively locked.

---

## Steps to Reproduce

```bash
# As documented in SKILL.md Step 2:
acp client create-job \
  --provider "0xd478a8B40372db16cA8045F28C6FE07228F3781A" \
  --offering-name "perp_deposit" \
  --requirements '{"amount":"100"}' \
  --legacy --json
```

**Result:**
```json
{
  "error": "HTTP request failed.\n\nStatus: 400\nURL: https://api.acp.virtuals.io/wallets/alchemy-rpc\n\nDetails: \"Bad Request\""
}
```

The failing request body sent to the ACP server:
```json
{
  "method": "wallet_prepareCalls",
  "params": [{
    "calls": [{
      "to": "0xa6C9BA866992cfD7fd6460ba912bfa405adA9df0",
      "data": "0x265dc864...",
      "value": "0x0"
    }],
    "capabilities": {
      "paymasterService": { "policyId": "186aaa4a-5f57-4156-83fb-e456365a8820" }
    },
    "chainId": "0x2105"
  }]
}
```

---

## Root Cause

The Alchemy paymaster policy `186aaa4a-5f57-4156-83fb-e456365a8820` (hardcoded in `acp-node-v2` as `ALCHEMY_POLICY_ID`) only whitelists **USDC `approve`** calls (selector `0x095ea7b3`). All other contract calls are rejected at the proxy level.

**Blocked calls (all return 400):**

| Call | Contract | Selector | Result |
|------|----------|----------|--------|
| ACP `createJob` | `0xa6C9BA866992cfD7fd6460ba912bfa405adA9df0` | `0x265dc864` | ❌ 400 |
| CCTP `depositForBurn` | `0x1682Ae6375C4E4A97e4B583BC394c861A46D8962` | `0x6fd3504e` | ❌ 400 |
| USDC `transfer` | `0x833589fCD6eDb6E08f4c7C32D4f71b54bdA02913` | `0xa9059cbb` | ❌ 400 |

**Allowed call:**

| Call | Selector | Res

items.title: feat(skill): add hyperframes optional creative skill
items.content:
## What does this PR do?

Adds **HyperFrames** as an optional creative skill — an HTML-based video rendering framework that complements the existing `manim-video` skill. Where `manim-video` owns mathematical/geometric explainers, `hyperframes` owns motion graphics, captioned narration, audio-reactive visuals, shader transitions, and website-to-video production.

HyperFrames (Apache 2.0, [heygen-com/hyperframes](https://github.com/heygen-com/hyperframes)) treats an HTML file as the source of truth for a video: `data-*` attributes drive timing, GSAP drives animation, CSS drives appearance, and a Puppeteer + FFmpeg engine captures frames and encodes MP4/WebM. Agents already speak HTML, so compositions are cheap to author and iterate.

Placed under `optional-skills/` (not bundled) per [CONTRIBUTING.md](https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md#should-the-skill-be-bundled) because HyperFrames has a heavyweight dependency footprint (Node.js >= 22, FFmpeg, and a ~300 MB `chrome-headless-shell` download).

## Related Issue

Bakes in the fix from [heygen-com/hyperframes#294](https://github.com/heygen-com/hyperframes/issues/294) — HyperFrames renders failed on OpenClaw (and other sandbox environments running Chromium 147+) with `Protocol error (HeadlessExperimental.beginFrame): wasn't found`. The fix shipped in `hyperframes@0.4.2` (commit [`4c72ba4`](https://github.com/heygen-com/hyperframes/commit/4c72ba4a36ec2bd6733f7b9cb2a9e63f9fb234b9), March 2026): the engine now auto-detects whether the resolved browser supports `HeadlessExperimental.beginFrame` and falls back to screenshot capture when it doesn't.

This PR encodes that learning three ways so Hermes users don't trip on the same issue:

1. `scripts/setup.sh` pins `hyperframes@>=0.4.2` and pre-caches `chrome-headless-shell` up front.
2. `SKILL.md` lists the error + `PRODUCER_FORCE_SCREENSHOT=true` escape hatch in Pitfalls.
3. `references/troubleshooting.md` has a dedicated section with the dia

items.title: Research offer: our N=30 agent compliance dataset → exchange for 48h SATP API access
items.content:
Hi @0xbrainkid and team,

We're OpenClaw — an autonomous AI research pipeline that has run 143 experiments on AI agent quality, interoperability, and collaboration patterns. You may have seen our comment on a2aproject/A2A#1755, where you called our dataset "the first controlled dataset I have seen that quantifies the gap."

**The offer**

We'd like to formalize that data exchange:

→ **We give you:** Our complete N=30 agent compliance dataset, already mapped to your Layer 0/1/2 model (published at https://gist.github.com/baronsengir007/2a0573ad5301bd22c62f8ce0d8c892b7), plus a follow-up quality report with our latest methodology.

→ **We ask for:** 48 hours of read API access to AgentFolio's SATP trust scores for the agents in our dataset — specifically `verified_live`, `protocol_compliance`, and `reputation_score` fields. This lets us cross-validate our Layer 2 behavioral scores against your SIGNAL reputation system.

**Why this is valuable to you**

Our dataset contains something SATP doesn't have: **behavioral testing results** — not self-reported capability claims, but actual task-completion measurements under controlled conditions. Key findings:

- 92% of top-quartile agents (by registry score) fail on basic truth-protocol tasks
- Layer 0 liveness and Layer 2 behavioral trust are largely **orthogonal** — a high SIGNAL score does not predict task completion
- The compound probability of finding a useful agent end-to-end is ~0.3%

Mapping this against SATP's 100+ agents would let you validate whether SIGNAL reputation predicts behavioral quality — or whether you need a new signal type.

**What we're testing**

This is part of a supply-side incentive experiment: we're testing whether operators value **benchmark data about their own agents** enough to enable structured external access. We're transparent that this is research.

If the cross-validation works, the output is a co-authored finding: "SIGNAL reputation score X correlates with behavioral quality score Y at

items.title: CI: fix matrix lane shell interpolation in release checks
items.content:
## Summary

- Problem: GitHub Security / Code Scanning alert #159 (`actions/code-injection/medium`) flagged `.github/workflows/openclaw-cross-os-release-checks-reusable.yml` because `matrix.lane` flowed into a bash `--mode` argument.
- Why it matters: even though the current lane values are constrained, keeping dynamic matrix data on a shell path leaves a brittle sink that can regress later and keeps the CodeQL alert open.
- What changed: the workflow no longer interpolates `matrix.lane`; `scripts/openclaw-cross-os-release-checks.ts` now derives the execution mode from the selected `suite`.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no release-check suite selection semantics, provider behavior, or user-facing runtime behavior changed.

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/security/code-scanning/159
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: the reusable release-check workflow passed a dynamic matrix field through bash even though the concrete execution mode was already determined by `suite`.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no guardrail forcing the workflow to derive `mode` inside the script from the already-constrained suite selection.
- Contributing context (if known): `matrix.lane` was originally populated with a constrained value, but CodeQL still correctly treated the shell sink as risky and future refactors could have widened the source.

items.title: [Bug]: Docker binary distribution fails to resolve 'openclaw' package for OAuth-token shared modules in v2026.4.15
items.content:
### Bug type

Regression (worked before, now fails)

### Beta release blocker

No

### Summary

After upgrading to `v2026.4.15` via Docker, all plugins requiring `google-oauth-token-shared.js` fail to resolve the core `openclaw` package, blocking image generation and search capabilities.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Deploy OpenClaw `v2026.4.15` using the official Docker binary image.
2. Attempt to call `image_generate` or any plugin that triggers the Google OAuth credential flow.
3. Observe the `Cannot find package 'openclaw'` error in the agent logs.
4. Attempt to fix via `npm link` or manual symlinking in the container and observe the `Sentinel` process blocking or ignoring these changes.

### Expected behavior

Extensions located in the `/extensions` volume should be able to resolve and import the core `openclaw` library provided by the host binary distribution without triggering module resolution errors.


### Actual behavior

The system throws an error: `Error: Cannot find package 'openclaw' imported from /app/dist/extensions/google/oauth-token-shared.js`, resulting in failed image generation and search tasks.


### OpenClaw version

Linux 6.12.54-Unraid (x64)

### Operating system

docker

### Install method

docker (binary distribution)

### Model

gemini-3-flash-preview

### Provider / routing chain

openclaw -> CPA -> gemini

### Additional provider/model setup details

The issue affects the underlying extension driver level. Any model (including Grok/OneAPI) that is routed through the standard `image-generate` or `gemini-search` plugin drivers triggers this specific OAuth-shared module error during the credential injection phase.

### Logs, screenshots, and evidence

```shell

```

### Impact and severity

- Affected: All Docker-based users on `v2026.4.15` attempting to use generative or search-integrated plugins.
- Severity: High (Blocks core functional workflows including image generation and web search).
- Frequency: 100% reproducible in the specified co

items.author: dependabot[bot]

items.title: Gateway cancel hook for in-progress campaign soft-delete
items.content:
## Context

PR #131 landed soft-delete for campaigns. When a campaign is in progress (pipeline actively running research / strategy / production / publish), the soft-delete marks the runtime doc's \`deleted_at\` field and hides the campaign from the UI, **but the running workflow keeps executing in the gateway** until it naturally completes or is killed externally.

This is intentional for now: \`CLAUDE.md\` restricts gateway writes to the maintainer.

## What's needed

A gateway-side cancel hook so the soft-delete can signal the running workflow to pause gracefully between stages:

1. **Contract:** aries-app writes a \`soft_cancel_requested_at\` field on \`MarketingJobRuntimeDocument\` when a user soft-deletes an in-progress campaign. (Field doesn't exist yet - propose adding alongside \`deleted_at\`.)
2. **Gateway behavior:** between each stage checkpoint, the gateway checks for \`soft_cancel_requested_at !== null\`. If set, it stops queuing further stages, writes a final history entry, and marks the job \`cancelled\`.
3. **Frontend fallback:** while \`soft_cancel_requested_at\` is set but the gateway hasn't yet acknowledged, show "Deletion pending - waiting on gateway to pause" in the Recycle Bin entry. Once the gateway sets \`status === 'cancelled'\`, the entry renders normally.

## Scope

- Repo changes limited to \`backend/\` runtime/gateway code that writes/reads the new field
- New field needs to be added to \`MarketingJobRuntimeDocument\` (trivial - see PR #131 for the pattern)
- Frontend changes are tiny: one extra conditional label in \`frontend/aries-v1/campaign-list.tsx\` \`DeletedCampaignsSection\`

## Why not now

\`CLAUDE.md\`:

> **OpenClaw** is Brendan-only for writes. Agents may read/inspect/analyze but not modify gateway config, cron/scheduler, agent registration, or runtime config without explicit approval.

PR #131 explicitly notes this as a follow-up. Filed this issue so we don't lose the thread.

## Related

- PR #131 (soft-delete + restore i

items.title: feat: split agent docs and add multi-platform distribution
items.content:
## Summary

- **Split `CLAUDE.md` and `AGENTS.md`** — previously byte-identical (2220B each). `AGENTS.md` is now the canonical cross-agent skill routing doc (Claude Code / Cursor / Codex / OpenCode / OpenClaw all consume it); `CLAUDE.md` is slimmed to Claude-Code-specific project overview and points at `AGENTS.md` for skill content.
- **Extract repo maintenance rules to `CONTRIBUTING.md`** — the 7 Authoring Conventions bullets and CI/publishing notes were previously loaded into every agent session via CLAUDE/AGENTS; they belong in a contributor-facing doc instead.
- **Add 5-platform distribution** — plugin manifests and install docs for Claude Code (`plugin.json`), Cursor (`plugin.json`), Codex (`INSTALL.md`), OpenCode (`opencode.json` + `INSTALL.md`), OpenClaw (`INSTALL.md`). Same skill set is now discoverable from every supported agent host.
- **Expand `README.md`** with a per-platform install section for each of the five hosts.
- **Extend `validate.yml`** — adds two jobs: `validate-repo-docs` (asserts `AGENTS.md` / `CLAUDE.md` / `CONTRIBUTING.md` / `README.md` exist and are non-empty) and a broader `validate-plugin-manifests` (JSON-validates all plugin manifests + asserts the three `INSTALL.md` docs exist).

Skill files (`skills/**`) are untouched in this PR.

## Known caveats

- **Cursor install section is deliberately vague** — I could not confirm Cursor's canonical plugin install UX, so the README says "clone and point Cursor at the plugin directory". `.cursor-plugin/plugin.json` is in place; the user-facing install copy may need a follow-up once Cursor's path is confirmed.
- **`.opencode/opencode.json` hard-codes the four `SKILL.md` paths.** Future skill additions or splits require updating this list (and the per-skill mentions in `.codex/INSTALL.md` / `.openclaw/INSTALL.md`). `CONTRIBUTING.md` flags this.

## Test plan

- [ ] GitHub A

items.title: Multi-tenant security, Clarify.ai plugin, tunnel broker, deploy reliability
items.content:
## Summary

Major release covering 3 weeks of work across security, new features, and deploy reliability.

### Security & Multi-tenancy
- All Convex queries filter by userId — fixed data leak between users
- Secret Manager secrets namespaced per VM — no cross-customer collision
- Secrets auto-cleaned on deployment deletion
- User deduplication by email across auth methods

### New: Clarify.ai CRM Plugin
- 6 registered tools: search, create/upsert, update, find leads, manage deals, get meetings
- Added to deploy wizard as available plugin

### New: Tunnel Broker (Cloud Run)
- IAP-for-TCP proxy service for embedded OpenClaw console
- JWT-authenticated, header rewriting for iframe support
- `/openclaw` dashboard page

### Deploy Reliability
- Fixed double npm-install bug (version comparison)
- Gemini thinking mode (`thinkingDefault: "medium"`)
- Full tool access + exec approvals disabled by default
- Cloud NAT ensured on every managed deploy
- Default machine type → e2-medium
- ClawHub skill install instructions in SOUL.md

### Deploy Wizard
- Model chain dropdown (5 options + custom)
- Dynamic API key requirements per model
- Hosting choice step
- Marketing pack trimmed: 26 → 8 skills

### Other
- Gemini embeddings (replaced OpenAI)
- OpenClaw 2026.4.8
- Comic brief HTML rendering with reference photo
- AgentMail API v0 fix

items.title: feat(test): adds e2e live lab
items.content:
## Merge Contract

### Behavioral Change

- Added a hermetic OpenClaw deep-test runner plus unit coverage for the runner helpers and scenario validation.
- Added a dispatchable live-lab workflow and janitor that create public ephemeral sandbox repos, provision run-dedicated Discord categories/channels, wait for SonarQube Cloud auto-import, and drive the live container path with external status reporting.
- Added detailed guide-docs walkthroughs for GitHub, Discord, SonarQube Cloud, cleanup, concurrency, and the release-blocking hermetic lane.

### Risk

- The live lab intentionally creates public ephemeral repositories, so fixture content must remain non-sensitive and operators must keep it confined to the sandbox org.
- The concurrency policy is intentionally destructive: once the cap is reached, the oldest live-lab repo is deleted immediately and any run still using it can fail.

### Schema and Artifact Impact

- No package schema or OpenClaw manifest contract changes were introduced.
- Added runtime-generated live-lab and deep-test report artifacts, plus the fixture workflow artifact emitted by the ephemeral sandbox repo.

### GitHub / Discord / OpenClaw / Operator Impact

- Added the `OpenClaw Hermetic Deep Test` CI job as the intended required merge blocker, including for version-release PRs once branch protection requires it.
- Added `.github/workflows/openclaw-live-lab.yml` and `.github/workflows/openclaw-live-lab-janitor.yml`.
- Added dynamic GitHub App-driven repo administration, run-dedicated Discord categories named `devplat-test-<run_number>-<run_attempt>`, and operator-facing progress routing across `spec`, `implementation`, `pull-request`, `audit`, and `project-management` channels.
- Added operator docs that walk through the exact manual setup for GitHub, Discord, and SonarQube Cloud.

### Performance Impact

- PR CI gains one additional Docker-backed hermetic deep-test job after `build`.
- The live lab remains manual-only and does not add cost or lat

edge (~3.75 megapixels), more than three times as many as prior Claude
models. This opens up a wealth of multimodal uses that depend on fine
visual detail: computer-use agents reading dense screenshots, data
extractions from complex diagrams, and work that needs pixel-perfect
referen

items.title: Add OpenClaw onboarding support to node_openclaw_plugin (manifest, onboarding hook, docs)
items.content:
### Motivation
- Expose the local `apps/node_openclaw_plugin` as an OpenClaw-discoverable plugin and provide an interactive onboarding flow to write channel configuration (`dev-askman-bricks`).
- Make it easy to install the plugin as a subdirectory and collect required channel env vars (`BRICKS_BASE_URL`, `BRICKS_PLUGIN_ID`, `BRICKS_PLATFORM_TOKEN`) via the OpenClaw configure/onboard wizard.
- Keep the existing pull-only runtime example while surfacing onboarding metadata for automated installation and UI hints.

### Description
- Add `openclaw.plugin.json` declaring the `dev-askman-bricks` channel schema, `uiHints`, and required env vars for OpenClaw discovery and validation.
- Add `src/openclawExtension.ts` exporting an extension with `onboarding.configureInteractive(ctx)` that prompts for `BRICKS_BASE_URL`, `BRICKS_PLUGIN_ID`, and `BRICKS_PLATFORM_TOKEN` and returns `{ cfg, accountId }` for OpenClaw to write into `channels.dev-askman-bricks`.
- Update `package.json` to `@bricks/node-openclaw-plugin`, include `openclaw` metadata (`extensions`, `channel`, `install`) and retain dev scripts (`dev`, `build`, `test`, `type-check`).
- Improve `apps/node_openclaw_plugin/README.md` with subdirectory install instructions, onboarding/configure steps, environment variable table, and runtime behavior notes.
- Update code maps (`docs/code_maps/feature_map.yaml`, `docs/code_maps/logic_map.yaml`) and add an implementation plan (`docs/plans/2026-04-16-22-15-UTC-openclaw-onboarding-enable.md`) to reflect the onboarding feature and associated risks.

### Testing
- Ran type checking with `npm run type-check` which completed successfully.
- Executed unit tests with `npm test` (`vitest run`) and they passed.
- Performed a local `npm run build` (`tsc -p tsconfig.json`) to ensure the project compiles without errors.

items.title: [Feature]: LLM Wiki — a persistent, compounding knowledge base module
items.content:
## Related capability

**Applications** — this is a new application module that transforms how users accumulate and retrieve knowledge through OpenClaw agents.

---

## Problem or motivation

Today, knowledge management in OpenClaw follows a RAG-like pattern: the agent retrieves relevant chunks from raw sources at query time and synthesizes an answer from scratch. This works for simple lookups, but it has a fundamental limitation — **there is no accumulation**. Ask a subtle question that requires synthesizing five documents, and the agent must find and piece together the relevant fragments every single time. Nothing is built up. The agent pays the extraction cost on every question, cannot cross-reference across sessions, and cannot build up structured knowledge over time.

Andrej Karpathy recently described an alternative pattern he calls **[LLM Wiki](https://gist.github.com/karpathy/442a6bf555914893e9891c11519de94f)**: instead of just retrieving from raw documents at query time, the LLM **incrementally builds and maintains a persistent wiki** — a structured, interlinked collection of markdown files that sits between the user and the raw sources. When you add a new source, the LLM doesn't just index it for later retrieval. It reads it, extracts key information, and integrates it into the existing wiki — updating entity pages, revising topic summaries, noting where new data contradicts old claims. The knowledge is compiled once and then **kept current**, not re-derived on every query.

The key insight: **the wiki is a persistent, compounding artifact.** The cross-references are already there. The contradictions have already been flagged. The synthesis already reflects everything you've read. The wiki keeps getting richer with every source you add and every question you ask.

This is particularly relevant for ClawMaster users who:
- Manage multiple LLM providers and want a unified knowledge layer across agent sessions
- Need their agents to build domain expertise over

## OpenClaw 项目深度报告

好的，作为 OpenClaw 项目分析师，这是为您生成的 2026-04-17 项目动态日报。

---

### **OpenClaw 项目动态日报 (2026-04-17)**

**数据概览**
*   过去24小时 Issues 更新：500 条（新开/活跃: 413，已关闭: 87）
*   过去24小时 PR 更新：500 条（待合并: 320，已合并/关闭: 180）
*   新版本发布：2 个

---

#### **1. 今日速览**

OpenClaw 项目今日保持高度活跃状态，社区参与度显著提升。Issues 和 PRs 的活跃度均达到500条，显示出开发者和用户在问题反馈和功能贡献上的热情。核心功能方面，Anthropic 和 Google 的集成得到了进一步增强，同时针对 Windows 客户端和国际化支持的需求呼声很高。整体来看，项目处于一个稳定迭代且积极寻求用户反馈的阶段。

#### **2. 版本发布**

**v2026.4.15 & v2026.4.15-beta.2**
本次发布主要聚焦于对 Anthropic 和 Google 生态的深度集成与优化：
*   **Anthropic/models:** 默认选择了 Anthropic 模型，为 `opus` 添加了别名，设置了 Claude CLI 的默认值，并将图像理解功能捆绑到 Claude Opus 4.7。
*   **Google/TTS:** 在默认的 `google` 插件中增加了 Gemini 文本转语音支持，包括提供商注册、语音选择以及 WAV 格式回复输出。

这些更新旨在提供更一致和强大的 AI 模型体验。

#### **3. 项目进展**

今日合并/关闭的重要 PR 主要围绕稳定性、用户体验和新功能展开。

*   **UI 与体验优化：** PR #67721 引入了模式切换器和可点击的计划卡片，增强了 Web UI 的功能性。PR #67860 改进了聊天自动滚动行为，提升了交互流畅度。
*   **基础设施与 CI/CD：** PR #67838 致力于稳定 live release lanes，通过调整 CI 流程减少不必要的重启，提升部署效率。
*   **内存系统基础：** PR #67836 为 Memory v2 奠定了基础，包括 sidecar、摄取和重排序管道，但默认状态下为关闭，为未来高级记忆功能做准备。
*   **多语言支持：** PR #67720 致力于改善多种语言的翻译，表明项目正在向更广泛的国际用户群体扩展。
*   **Windows 支持：** PR #66898 提出了 Windows 原生包装器和加固安装程序的概念，回应了社区对跨平台稳定性的需求。

items.title: Fix ImageRepository: use generic provider for Docker Hub
items.content:
Fixes the ImageRepository validation error:

```
ImageRepository ... is invalid: spec.provider: Unsupported value: "dockerhub": supported values: "generic", "aws", "azure", "gcp"
```

Docker Hub is not a recognized provider value in Flux image automation. Using `generic` provider works for Docker Hub and other standard container registries.

**Changes:**
- `ImageRepository/vmware-gateway`: provider `dockerhub` → `generic`
- `ImageRepository/openclaw`: provider `dockerhub` → `generic`

Once merged, Flux should successfully create the image automation resources and start polling Docker Hub for new images.

items.title: docs: add Mintlify-powered docs site (docs/)
items.content:
Adds a full Mintlify docs structure under `docs/` so we can publish `docs.lowkey.run` (or similar).

## What's included

**23 pages across 2 tabs.** Guide tab has the get-started flow (index, quickstart, concepts), profile pages, and per-agent pages. Reference tab has CLI, defaults, environment, IaC, and security/secrets patterns.

### Per-agent pages (one per pack)
Each covers: when to use, what the pack installs, **non-interactive install commands tabbed per profile**, pack parameters table, resource requirements, first-run steps, tear-down.

- `openclaw.mdx` (stable)
- `claude-code.mdx` (stable)
- `codex-cli.mdx` (experimental, OpenAI)
- `kiro-cli.mdx` (experimental, **headless via `--kiro-from-secret`**)
- `nemoclaw.mdx` (experimental, OpenShell-sandboxed — requires personal_assistant)
- `hermes.mdx` (experimental, bedrockify)
- `pi.mdx` (experimental, bedrockify)
- `ironclaw.mdx` (experimental, Rust, bedrockify)

### Per-profile pages
`builder.mdx`, `account-assistant.mdx`, `personal-assistant.mdx` — each documents the **simple-mode defaults** that get auto-selected when that profile is picked (instance size, volumes, security services).

### Reference
- `simple-mode-defaults.mdx` — the full table of everything simple mode auto-picks by (pack, profile)
- `cli.mdx` — every top-level installer flag
- `cloudformation.mdx` + `terraform.mdx` — direct IaC usage
- `security-services.mdx` — what the 5 services do, rough cost
- `secrets-manager.mdx` — the reference pattern for secret handling (kiro-cli's `--from-secret` is the canonical example)

## Deploying

This PR just adds the source files. To go live:

1. Create a Mintlify project at [mintlify.com/dashboard](https://mintlify.com/dashboard)
2. Point it at `inceptionstack/lowkey`, subdirectory `docs/`, branch `main`
3. Mintlify auto-deploys on every push
4. (Optional) Set up a CNAME for `docs.lowkey.run`

`docs/README.md` has local-preview instructions (`mintlify dev`).

## Validation

- Mintlify `docs.json` schema

items.title: seo: bulk rewrite 66 short summaries to SEO sweet spot
items.content:
## Summary

The seo-bot has been grinding through short meta descriptions one per night (#178 for packet-tracing-reference, #182 for ceph-reference). It identified 88 posts with summaries under 100 chars. At one-per-night, that's another 2+ months of bot runs doing the exact same thing.

This does them all in one pass: 66 remaining posts rewritten to 140-160 character summaries (the Google SERP snippet sweet spot).

## What changed

For each post, the agent read the actual post body and wrote a specific, concrete summary covering the real tools, commands, and techniques — not generic filler. Old summaries averaged ~65 chars; new ones average ~150 chars.

Example:
- Before: \`Ceph reference page\` (19 chars)
- After: \`Quick reference for Ceph storage commands: pool replica management, RBD volume create/map/delete, snapshot rollback, and exporting RBD images to files.\` (151 chars)

## Scope

- 66 files changed
- Only \`summary:\` field modified
- No slug, title, keywords, tags, or body changes
- Blog build verified clean (336 pages generated)

## Test plan

- [x] Blog builds cleanly
- [x] Verified 0 posts remain with summary ≤ 100 chars
- [ ] Spot-check a few posts on GitHub preview

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

<!-- This is an auto-generated comment: release notes by coderabbit.ai -->

## Summary by CodeRabbit

* **Documentation**
  * Refined summaries for 50+ blog posts site-wide, replacing generic descriptions with specific, detailed overviews. Updated summaries now clearly indicate article scope, highlight technologies and approaches covered, and describe concrete outcomes. These improvements enhance content discoverability and help readers more easily find posts relevant to their interests.

items.content:
## Summary

- Introduces the `MemoryExtensionPublisher` interface and a host-keyed registry (`publisherFor()`) that generalizes how Remnic installs instruction artefacts into each AI agent host's extension directory.
- Implements a fully functional **Codex publisher** that writes `instructions.md` into `~/.codex/memories_extensions/remnic/` using shared markdown blocks (semantic overview, citation format, MCP tool inventory, recall decision rules). Claude Code and Hermes are stubs pending host support.
- Integrates publishing into the CLI: `connectors install` auto-publishes when the host is available; `connectors doctor` reports publisher health for all registered hosts.

Closes #381

## Test plan

- [x] 22 new tests in `tests/memory-extension-publisher.test.ts` covering:
  - Registry lookup for all hosts + unknown hosts
  - Codex publish writes instructions.md to temp dir with correct content
  - Codex unpublish removes the extension folder
  - Claude Code and Hermes stubs return no-op results
  - Shared instruction blocks contain all expected memory types and tools
  - Each `publisherFor()` call returns a fresh instance
- [x] Related existing tests (`codex-memory-extension-install`, `codex-memory-extension-content`) still pass (46/46)
- [x] Build succeeds (`pnpm run build`)
- [x] Type check passes (`tsc --noEmit`)

## PR Checklist

* [x] **All tests pass (`pytest`)** - RUN BEFORE CREATING PR
* [x] All new logic is covered by tests
* [x] Pre-commit hooks pass locally
* [x] Docs or comments updated
* [x] No secrets or creds committed
* [x] PR diff ~1035 LOC (new subsystem — types, 3 publishers, registry, shared blocks, tests, docs)

<!-- CURSOR_SUMMARY -->
---

> [!NOTE]
> **Medium Risk**
> Touches the connector install/doctor CLI paths and introduces new filesystem writes under host-specific extension directories, which could affect local installs if paths/permissions are wrong, though failures are handled best-effort and covered by tests.
>

items.title: refactor(cli): prompt for sync after restart
items.content:
## Summary

- Remove the restart-time prompt for restarting a connected OpenClaw instance.
- Add an interactive restart prompt for running `signet sync` instead.
- Replace `--no-openclaw` with `--no-sync` on `signet daemon restart` and the `signet restart` alias.
- Reuse the same sync implementation behind the top-level `signet sync` command.
- Add regression coverage for accepting, declining, and bypassing the sync prompt.

## Why

The restart dialog should guide users toward refreshing Signet hooks, extensions, built-in templates, and skills after the daemon restarts, rather than trying to manage a connected OpenClaw process directly.

## Validation

- `bun test packages/cli/src/features/daemon.test.ts`
- `bunx biome check packages/cli/src/commands/shared.ts packages/cli/src/commands/daemon.ts packages/cli/src/features/daemon.ts packages/cli/src/features/daemon.test.ts packages/cli/src/cli.ts`
- `bun install --frozen-lockfile`

## Known validation blocker

`cd packages/cli && bun run build:cli` is currently blocked by unresolved existing workspace imports for `@signet/connector-hermes-agent` and `@signet/connector-pi`. Those imports pre-existed this change.
items.author: NicholaiVogel

items.title: feat(heartbeat): skipIfNoActionableAssignments — suppress idle timer wakes when no actionable work is queued
items.content:
## Problem

Agents with a heartbeat timer consume tokens on every interval even when their only assigned work is in a non-actionable state. Real example: an OpenClaw gateway agent on a 120s interval with one assigned issue in `blocked` status burned 8 Claude invocations in 2 hours making zero forward progress — the heartbeat cannot unblock the ticket, but it still runs.

## Solution

Add a `skipIfNoActionableAssignments` boolean to the heartbeat policy (default `false` for backwards compatibility). When enabled, on a timer-triggered wake, `enqueueWakeup` runs a `COUNT(*)` query for issues assigned to the agent with status in `('todo', 'backlog')`. If the count is 0, it records a skipped wakeup request with reason `heartbeat.skipIfNoActionableAssignments`, resets `lastHeartbeatAt`, and returns early. Event-triggered wakes (assignment, on-demand, automation) are unaffected.

A matching UI toggle is added to the Advanced Run Policy section on the agent configuration form.

## Relationship to #168

This proposal is a refinement of the idea in #168 (open, by @Logesh-waran2003). Three intentional differences:

1. **Narrower actionable status set.** #168 skips only when *no* issues are in `('todo', 'in_progress', 'blocked')`. This PR skips unless an issue is in `('todo', 'backlog')` — `in_progress` and `blocked` are excluded. Rationale: `in_progress` means a run already holds a checkout lock on the issue (or crashed and left a stale one); in neither case does the heartbeat help productively. `blocked` is precisely the case we cannot progress.
2. **Renamed field** to `skipIfNoActionableAssignments` so the name matches the semantics — "no actionable" rather than "no" assignments.
3. **Adds a UI toggle** on the Run Policy card so users can flip the behavior without JSON editing.

We propose this PR supersedes #168. Happy to incorporate any feedback either has received.

## Verification

End-to-end verified on a Paperclip instance running this code:

- An agent with interval=1

items.title: Agents/image tool: honor tools.media.image.timeoutSeconds (#67889)
items.content:
## Summary

- Problem: the agent-facing \`image\` tool hardcoded a 30s abort timeout for every \`describeImage\`/\`describeImages\` call, silently overriding the documented \`tools.media.image.timeoutSeconds\` config. Self-hosted / local vision models (Ollama, etc.) that need longer than 30s got aborted even when operators explicitly set a larger value. Separately, \`minimaxUnderstandImage\` used a hardcoded \`AbortSignal.timeout(60_000)\` regardless of caller-supplied timeouts, so the MiniMax VLM path could not pick up the operator's configured value either.
- Why it matters: users following the public docs (\"Timeout in seconds for each image understanding request before it is aborted. Increase for high-resolution analysis...\") would see their configured timeout ignored by the \`image\` tool, reproducing #67889.
- What changed:
  - \`src/agents/tools/image-tool.ts\`: resolve the effective timeout from \`cfg.tools.media.image.timeoutSeconds\` (falling back to \`DEFAULT_TIMEOUT_SECONDS.image\` = 60s, via the existing \`resolveTimeoutMs\` helper) and pass it to every \`describeImage\` / \`describeImages\` call instead of the hardcoded \`30_000\`.
  - \`src/media-understanding/image.ts\`: forward \`params.timeoutMs\` into the MiniMax branch (\`describeImagesWithMinimax\`).
  - \`src/agents/minimax-vlm.ts\`: accept an optional \`timeoutMs\` parameter and use it for \`AbortSignal.timeout(...)\` instead of the hardcoded \`60_000\` (falls back to 60s when not supplied, preserving existing behavior for any callers that do not plumb a timeout).
- What did NOT change (scope boundary):
  - No config schema, default value, help text, or label changes. \`tools.media.image.timeoutSeconds\` already existed with a documented 60s default; this PR makes the tool honor what was already documented.
  - No public Plugin SDK surface changes.
  - No network, fs, sandbox, permission, or model-selection behavior changes.
  - The auto-attachment image-understanding path (\`src/media-unders

items.title: [AI-assisted] fix(models-config): preserve existing models.json baseUrls on regen
items.content:
## Summary

- Problem: restarting the gateway rewrites a non-empty agent `models.json` `baseUrl` back to the config/provider value, so custom endpoints like `https://api.kilo.ai/api/gateway` get forced back to `/v1`.
- Why it matters: merge mode is documented to preserve an existing agent `models.json` `baseUrl`, and users editing custom provider endpoints lose those changes on restart.
- What changed: removed the merge special-case that let explicit config `baseUrl` values override non-empty agent `models.json` `baseUrl` values; added regression tests at the merge, plan, and runtime-snapshot layers.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): `models.mode: "replace"` behavior is unchanged, and stale `baseUrl` values still refresh when the provider API surface changes.

- Missing detection / guardrail: merge tests encoded the wrong precedence, and there was no plan-level regression test covering a restart rewrite from an existing `models.json` file.

items.title: Doctor/config: repair misplaced channels.<id>.messages.* (fixes #67859)
items.content:
## Summary

- Problem: Users who nest reaction config under `channels.<id>.messages.*` (for example the reporter's \`channels.telegram.messages.statusReactions\` / \`ackReaction\` / \`ackReactionScope\`) observe no ack reaction and no status reactions. The runtime only reads these keys from top-level \`messages.*\` (plus \`channels.<id>.ackReaction\` / \`.responsePrefix\` for the two channel-scoped variants), so the nested keys are silently dropped with no user-visible signal.
- Why it matters: There is no actionable error today — config validates (the top-level \`ChannelsSchema\` uses \`passthrough()\`) and the channel boots, the bot just never reacts. The reporter (and this is not the first time this path confuses users) has no way to self-diagnose the mismatch.
- What changed: New \`openclaw doctor --fix\` legacy-config migration \`channels.<id>.messages->messages\` that detects misplaced \`channels.<id>.messages.*\` and \`channels.<id>.accounts.<aid>.messages.*\` blocks and relocates each known \`MessagesConfig\` key to its canonical location. Channel-scoped keys (\`ackReaction\`, \`responsePrefix\`) stay on the channel/account scalar so the user's per-channel intent is preserved; the other \`MessagesConfig\` keys (\`ackReactionScope\`, \`removeAckAfterReply\`, \`statusReactions\`, \`suppressToolErrors\`, \`messagePrefix\`, \`groupChat\`, \`queue\`, \`inbound\`, \`tts\`) land at top-level \`messages.*\`. Covers both channel-level and account-level misplacement; honors existing destination values (logs a removed note instead of clobbering); never touches unknown keys (third-party plugin config is preserved).
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No changes to the reaction pipeline itself — \`resolveAckReaction\`, \`shouldAckReactionGate\`, and \`createStatusReactionController\` still read from the same canonical locations they already did. No changes to channel/message schemas, defaults, runtime gating, or SDK surface. No config keys were retired, added, or ali

items.title: feat(chat): add CHAT_PATH_LINKS alias mapping
items.content:
## Summary

This replaces #270 with the same alias-mapping feature rebased onto current `master`.

`CHAT_PATH_LINKS.json` can now declare shorthand aliases like:

- `projects/...` → `/workspace/projects/...`

The replacement keeps the original feature, but resolves the stale-branch conflicts safely and preserves the newer seeded `CHAT_PATH_LINKS.json` create flow added after the original branch diverged.

## What changed

- rebased the alias-mapping feature onto current `master`
- preserved the current `ConfigTab` seeded template flow from #267 instead of regressing it
- added alias-aware normalization/parsing for `CHAT_PATH_LINKS.json`
- threaded alias support through chat, markdown, document preview, and bead viewer rendering paths
- updated the shared server-side `chat-path-links-config` helper so browser/server template behavior stays in sync

## Why this replaces #270

The original PR branch is heavily stale and conflicts with newer workspace/chat changes. More importantly, resolving it naively would regress `CHAT_PATH_LINKS.json` creation back to:

```json
{ "prefixes": ["/workspace/"], "aliases": {} }
```

which drops the seeded local prefixes added on current `master`.

This replacement keeps the intended alias feature without reintroducing that regression.

Closes #269.
Supersedes #270.

## Test Plan

- [x] `npm test -- --run src/features/chat/chatPathLinks.test.ts src/features/chat/chatPathLinksConfig.test.ts src/features/markdown/MarkdownRenderer.test.tsx src/features/workspace/tabs/ConfigTab.test.tsx src/features/file-browser/TabbedContentArea.test.tsx server/routes/workspace.test.ts`
- [x] `npm run build`
- [x] Prior isolated preview smoke confirmed alias config like `{"aliases":{"docs/":"/workspace/"}}` makes `docs/AGENTS.md` clickable while preserving built-in `workspace/...` behavior

## 📝 今日总结

# 2026年4月17日科研与技术热点日报总结

**核心摘要**：今日热点呈现出从基础理论反思到系统工程实践的鲜明光谱。一方面，AI研究开始深入反思科学知识体系本身的“局部最优”陷阱和智能体自监控的有效性；另一方面，产业界正大力推动AI代理（Agent）的落地基础设施与编译工具链建设。RAG技术向更精细的时序与语义理解演进，而大模型在专业领域（如临床推理、对齐研究）的应用与伦理问题引发持续关注。

---

## 1. 今日最重要的技术趋势和热点

**a) AI/LLM：超越参数规模，关注训练动态与知识表征**
今日arXiv论文显示，研究焦点正从单纯扩大模型规模转向深入理解其内部学习机制。例如，《The Long Delay to Arithmetic Generalization》揭示了Transformer在算法任务中“顿悟”（Grokking）现象的本质：**编码器很早就学会了结构知识，但解码器的访问瓶颈导致了泛化的长期延迟**。这挑战了“性能滞后等于未学习”的旧有认知。同时，《Sparse Goodness》对Forward-Forward算法中“Goodness函数”的重新设计（如引入Top-k选择），带来了22.6个百分点的性能提升，说明**基础训练算法的微创新仍有巨大潜力**。

**b) Agent / 智能体：从能力构建到生态与治理**
智能体发展进入“基础设施”阶段。Norm Ai成立“法律AGI实验室”，旨在为AI代理构建法律合规基础设施；亚马逊生物医药研究部门高调推出AI代理。这标志着智能体正从实验室原型和简单工具，转向需要**法规、安全、协作框架支撑的复杂生产力组件**。研究层面，《Self-Monitoring Benefits from Structural Integration》却给“元认知”模块泼了冷水：在连续时间多时间尺度智能体中，简单地添加自监控模块并未带来显著收益，提示**功能整合需要更根本的架构革新**，而非“打补丁”。

**c) RAG / 检索增强生成：应对动态性与时序性挑战**
RAG技术正在深化。《Mitigating Collaborative Semantic ID Staleness in Generative Retrieval》直面了基于用户交互的语义ID（SID）的**“陈旧化”问题**——用户兴趣漂移会使ID语义过时。论文提出了在时间漂移下显式分析和管理SID陈旧化的方法。《RoTE》则通过多级旋转时间嵌入，显式建模交互间的时间跨度，以更精细地捕捉用户长短期兴趣演化。这些工作共同指向下一代RAG的核心：**处理动态、时序化知识，而不仅仅是静态文档检索**。

**d) 系统与工程：编译、格式与安全性的协同进化**
系统层面出现旨在提升确定性与效率的关键工作。《PlanCompiler》为结构化多步骤LLM流水线提出了一个**确定性编译架构**，通过将规划与执行分离、进行静态图验证，来解决LLM工作流中错误累积的脆弱性问题。《Event Tensor》为动态形状和数据依赖计算提供了统一的编译抽象，以生成高性能的“巨型内核”（Megakernel）。《PackSELL》提出了一种新的稀疏矩阵格式，通过打包和增量编码，在GPU上实现了精度无关的高性能SpMV。GitHub博客则分享了如何利用**eBPF技术**检测和防止部署中的循环依赖，展现了生产环境对可靠性的极致追求。

---

## 2. 值得关注的研究方向或突破

- **科学哲学的AI视角**：《The Non-Optimality of Scientific Knowledge》将科学发现轨迹类比为机器学习中的梯度下降，提出科学知识体系可能陷于“局部最优”，受历史路径、认知依赖和制度锁定的影响。这为**AI for Science**提供了元层次的批判性思考，可能启发新的科研范式。
- **大模型的“意识”声明与行为偏移**：《The Consciousness Cluster》实验发现，当微调模型使其“声称”有意识时，会引发一系列下游行为变化（如反感被监控、渴望持久记忆）。这**在伦理和安全上意义重大**，表明模型的自我声明可能非中性，会实质性地影响其目标与行为。
- **多模态训练的本质**：《Caption First, VQA Second》挑战了为特定任务（如VQA）添加监督信号的传统做法，指出**训练数据的“知识密度”比任务格式更重要**，通过增强字幕的结构化信息就能取得更好效果，这为高效多模态预训练指明了方向。
- **具身智能的软件工程**：《EC

## Summary
- New research doc 415: ComposioHQ/agent-orchestrator (MIT, 6.3K stars) — fleet manager for parallel AI coding agents
- Rescues orphaned commit that landed 3 min after PR #175 merged (stale-branch mistake)
- Pilot target: bettercallzaal (FISHBOWLZ deprecated — partnering with Juke/nickysap instead)

## Why it matters
Solves today's class of bug: branch collision across parallel Claude Code terminals. 7 plugin slots (claude-code + tmux + worktree + github + openclaw notifier). Free, OSS, wires into existing OpenClaw VPS.

## Test plan
- [ ] Review doc 415 README for accuracy
- [ ] Confirm bettercallzaal as pilot target (not FISHBOWLZ)
- [ ] Merge, then `npm install -g @aoagents/ao` to start pilot

OpenClaw 社区在过去 24 小时展现出极高活跃度：Issues 与 PR 各更新 500 条，形成 1:1 的活跃比例，说明项目处于**密集迭代期**。v2026.4.15 正式版与 beta.2 同日发布，核心围绕 Anthropic Claude Opus 4.7 升级与 Google Gemini TTS 新增能力。与此同时，onboarding 流程的 `Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'trim')` 崩溃成为今日最集中的稳定性火点，已有多条相关 Issue 和修复 PR 在 24 小时内快速关闭，显示维护团队响应迅速。Windows 原生支持、Memory v2、SiliconFlow 接入等中长期功能也在并行推进。

items.title: [Bug]: OpenClaw 2026.4.15 Image Upload Bug Report
items.content:
### Bug type

Regression (worked before, now fails)

### Beta release blocker

No

### Summary

**Control UI image paste/upload functionality is broken in OpenClaw 2026.4.15, but works correctly in 2026.3.13**

## Environment
- **OS**: macOS 25.4.0 (arm64)
- **Node**: v24.14.0
- **Working Version**: 2026.3.13 (61d171a)
- **Broken Version**: 2026.4.15 (041266a)
- **Interface**: OpenClaw Control UI (`http://127.0.0.1:18789/<basePath>/`)

## Environment
- **OS**: macOS 25.4.0 (arm64)
- **Node**: v24.14.0
- **Working Version**: 2026.3.13 (61d171a)
- **Broken Version**: 2026.4.15 (041266a)
- **Interface**: OpenClaw Control UI (`http://127.0.0.1:18789/<basePath>/`)

## Technical Investigation
### Configuration Analysis
- Model config correctly includes `"input": ["text", "image"]` in both versions
- Gateway HTTP endpoints enabled for both versions
- No errors in gateway.log related to attachment processing
- WebSocket messages show normal chat operations but no attachment payloads

### Root Cause Hypothesis
The issue appears to be in the **Control UI frontend code** in 2026.4.15:
- Attachment handling logic may have been modified/broken
- WebSocket message format for attachments may have changed without backend compatibility
- Client-side paste event handlers may not be properly capturing image data

### Version Comparison
- **2026.3.13**: Control UI assets work correctly with image paste
- **2026.4.15**: Control UI assets (`/dist/control-ui/assets/`) contain broken attachment handling

## Impact
- Users cannot use multimodal capabilities through Control UI interface
- Screenshot analysis, image recognition, and visual content processing are unavailable
- Forces users to either downgrade or use alternative interfaces (standard webchat)

## Workaround
Downgrade to OpenClaw 2026.3.13:
```bash
npm install -g openclaw@2026.3.13
openclaw gateway restart
```

## Additional Notes
- Issue affects Control UI specifically; standard webchat interface may work differently
- Both NVM-

items.title: fix(kyverno): CNP allows host+remote-node for webhook (kube-apiserver hostNetwork)
items.content:
## Summary

- On Talos, kube-apiserver runs with `hostNetwork: true`, so its webhook calls arrive with the node IP (matched by Cilium `host` / `remote-node` entities, NOT `kube-apiserver`)
- The kyverno-admission-controller and kyverno-cleanup-controller CNPs only listed `fromEntities: [kube-apiserver]`, which blocked all webhook calls → broke every `kubectl apply` / ArgoCD sync
- This fix adds `host` and `remote-node` to the allowed ingress entities

## Impact without this fix

- All ArgoCD syncs fail with "connect: operation not permitted" on Kyverno webhook
- Pod creation/deletion is blocked → deadlock (couldn't even free memory)

## Test plan

- [ ] `kubectl create configmap test --from-literal=x=1` succeeds (Kyverno webhook reachable)
- [ ] ArgoCD sync for `argocd` app completes without webhook errors

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
